categoryName: Pedigrees and Patterns of Monogenic Inheritance

questions.topicName: Pedigrees and Patterns of Monogenic Inheritance
questions.stem: The arrow in this pedigree marks the proband (filled circle). The degree symbols (1°, 2°, 3°, 4°) placed on other individuals indicate what?

choices.label: C
choices.text: The generation number of each individual

choices.label: D
choices.text: Each relative's degree of genetic relationship to the proband

choices.label: E
choices.text: The age at diagnosis for each relative

explanation.summary: First-degree relatives (parents, siblings, children) share ~50% of genes, second-degree ~25%, third-degree ~12.5%, and so on. Labeling relatives by degree relative to the proband is used to assess recurrence risk and inheritance pattern.
explanation.whyCorrect: First-degree relatives (parents, siblings, children) share ~50% of genes, second-degree ~25%, third-degree ~12.5%, and so on. Labeling relatives by degree relative to the proband is used to assess recurrence risk and inheritance pattern.

questions.topicName: Pedigrees and Patterns of Monogenic Inheritance
questions.stem: In this pedigree the trait appears in every generation, affects males and females roughly equally, and each affected person has an affected parent. What is the most likely mode of inheritance?

explanation.summary: Vertical transmission through every generation with both sexes affected and each affected individual having an affected parent is the hallmark of autosomal dominant inheritance. Male-to-male transmission would further exclude X-linkage.
explanation.whyCorrect: Vertical transmission through every generation with both sexes affected and each affected individual having an affected parent is the hallmark of autosomal dominant inheritance. Male-to-male transmission would further exclude X-linkage.

questions.topicName: Pedigrees and Patterns of Monogenic Inheritance
questions.stem: This pedigree marks many unaffected individuals as carriers (central dot), with affected individuals (filled) appearing in a single sibship born to phenotypically normal parents. What inheritance pattern is shown?

explanation.summary: Two unaffected heterozygous carriers producing affected children, with the trait skipping generations and affecting both sexes, is characteristic of autosomal recessive inheritance. Carrier status (dot) is emphasized because heterozygotes are clinically silent.
explanation.whyCorrect: Two unaffected heterozygous carriers producing affected children, with the trait skipping generations and affecting both sexes, is characteristic of autosomal recessive inheritance. Carrier status (dot) is emphasized because heterozygotes are clinically silent.

questions.topicName: Pedigrees and Patterns of Monogenic Inheritance
questions.stem: In this pedigree an affected father transmits the trait to all of his daughters but to none of his sons, and there is no male-to-male transmission. What is the most likely mode of inheritance?

explanation.summary: An affected male passes his single X to every daughter and his Y to every son, so in X-linked dominant inheritance all daughters and no sons of an affected father are affected. Absence of male-to-male transmission is the key clue.
explanation.whyCorrect: An affected male passes his single X to every daughter and his Y to every son, so in X-linked dominant inheritance all daughters and no sons of an affected father are affected. Absence of male-to-male transmission is the key clue.

questions.topicName: Pedigrees and Patterns of Monogenic Inheritance
questions.stem: An affected male's daughters are all unaffected carriers (dot), and affected males reappear in later generations through these carrier daughters, with no father-to-son transmission. What inheritance pattern does this represent?

explanation.summary: In X-linked recessive inheritance an affected male makes all daughters obligate carriers; those carrier daughters then transmit the allele to affected sons. The disorder predominantly affects males and skips through unaffected carrier females, with no male-to-male transmission.
explanation.whyCorrect: In X-linked recessive inheritance an affected male makes all daughters obligate carriers; those carrier daughters then transmit the allele to affected sons. The disorder predominantly affects males and skips through unaffected carrier females, with no male-to-male transmission.

questions.topicName: Pedigrees and Patterns of Monogenic Inheritance
questions.stem: The top cell in this figure contains a mixture of mutant (red) and normal (blue) mitochondria, which then randomly segregate to daughter cells. What term describes this coexistence of two mitochondrial genotypes within a single cell?

explanation.summary: Heteroplasmy is the presence of both mutant and wild-type mtDNA in one cell. Random segregation during division changes the mutant load; disease appears only once the mutant fraction exceeds a tissue-specific threshold for phenotypic expression, as shown by the disease vs normal phenotype branches.
explanation.whyCorrect: Heteroplasmy is the presence of both mutant and wild-type mtDNA in one cell. Random segregation during division changes the mutant load; disease appears only once the mutant fraction exceeds a tissue-specific threshold for phenotypic expression, as shown by the disease vs normal phenotype branches.
